title: Writer & Essayist
description: I write articles, essays, and technical guides about technology, philosophy, and the human experience.
heroIntro: I believe words are the most powerful technology we have. I write to think clearly, to share what I learn, and to make the complex feel simple.
about: I'm a developer who writes — not just code, but ideas. My essays sit at the crossroads of technology, philosophy, and everyday experience. I believe in slow reading, careful thinking, and writing that respects the reader's time.

epigraph.quote: The scariest moment is always just before you start.

essays.title: Understanding React 19 Server Components
essays.excerpt: Server components blur the line between the server and the browser. In this deep dive, I explore what that means for the way we think about rendering, state, and the future of the web.
essays.content: The React team has been working on server components for years. Now that they are here in React 19, it is worth taking a step back and asking: what problem do they actually solve?

essays.title: The Philosophy of Clean Code
essays.excerpt: Writing clean code is not merely a technical discipline — it is an ethical one. Every function you name, every abstraction you choose, is a message to the next person who reads it.
essays.content: There is a particular kind of pride a developer takes in a well-crafted function. Not pride of authorship, but something closer to the satisfaction of a sentence that says exactly what it means.

essays.title: On Learning in Public
essays.excerpt: Sharing your process before you have the answers is uncomfortable. It is also one of the most effective ways to learn, connect, and build something that lasts.
essays.content: I started writing publicly because I was afraid to. That fear, it turned out, was the exact reason I needed to do it.

essays.title: Why Developers Should Read More Fiction
essays.excerpt: Fiction teaches empathy. And empathy, as it turns out, is the most underrated engineering skill there is.
essays.content: The best systems I have ever worked with were designed by people who understood how other people think. Not algorithms — people.
